--- SOURCE 3: Phytoremediation strategies for mitigating environmental toxicants ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147

SUMMARY:
This study examines phytoremediation, a sustainable technology that uses plants to remove pollutants from soil, water, and air. The uptake of metals and organic compounds is a complex process influenced by the properties of the contaminants, the species of the plants, the characteristics of the soil, and the prevailing environmental conditions [5]. The field of phytoremediation is evolving through innovative techniques, including genetically modified bacteria and transgenic plants, which enhance the efficiency and specificity of remediation solutions (Mello-Farias et al., 2011) A sustainable remediation assessment evaluates the long-term impacts of phytoremediation projects on environmental, economic, and social resources. Phytoremediation is a sustainable approach for mitigating pollution in soil, water, and air by using plants to absorb, degrade, or transform contaminants.

Review article

Phytoremediation strategies for mitigating environmental toxicants
==================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Mahendra Aryal

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Phytoremediation is an eco-friendly technology for soil, water, and air pollution. 
*   •Addresses species selection, bacteria use, amendments, and genetic engineering. 
*   •Point out pollutant bioavailability and plant uptake mechanisms. 
*   •Emphasizes phytoremediation's role in effective environmental management. 
*   •Slow pollutant removal, limited plant species, emphasizing ongoing research. 

Abstract
--------

In natural environments, persistent pollutants such as heavy metals and organic compounds, are frequently sequestered in sediments, soils, and mineral deposits, rendering them biologically unavailable. This study examines phytoremediation, a sustainable technology that uses plants to remove pollutants from soil, water, and air. It discusses enhancing techniques such as plant selection, the use of plant growth-promoting bacteria, soil amendments, and genetic engineering. The study highlights the slow removal rates and the limited availability of plant species that are effective for specific pollutants. Furthermore, it investigates bioavailability and the mechanisms underlying root exudation and hyperaccumulation. Applications across diverse environments and innovative technologies, such as transgenic plants and nanoparticles, are also explored. Additionally, the potential for phytoremediation with bioenergy production is considered. The purpose of this study is to provide researchers, practitioners, and policymakers with valuable resources for sustainable solutions.

Phytoremediation

Sustainable technology

Contaminated site

Bioavailability

Environmental management

Bioenergy

1. Introduction
---------------

Phytoremediation, recognized as a sustainable and environmentally friendly approach, has garnered significant attention as a promising strategy for mitigating the impact of heavy metals and organic compounds on the environment. This innovative technique utilizes plants to naturally remove, detoxify, or immobilize contaminants, thereby contributing to the restoration of polluted ecosystems [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib1)]. Inorganic pollutants, such as heavy metals, metalloids, radionuclides, phosphates, and nitrates are widespread. While some heavy metals are essential for plant growth, excessive levels of lead, cadmium, mercury, and arsenic pose serious health risks to both humans and wildlife, leading to chronic anemia, neurological damage, developmental delays, kidney damage, cognitive impairment, and cancer [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib2)]. Organic pollutants resulting from industrial, urban, and agricultural activities also present substantial threats due to their persistent and harmful nature. Compounds like polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), phenols, and pesticides, due to their hydrophobic characteristics, persist in the environment and can bioaccumulate, causing toxicity in organisms and disrupting ecosystems (Singh and Jain, 2003).

Phytoremediation is a promising solution due to its ecological soundness, cost-effectiveness, and versatility in addressing various contaminants. Successful implementation requires understanding of the bioavailability of metals and organic compounds, which influence plant uptake and removal. This knowledge guides the selection of appropriate plant species and remediation strategies [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib3)]. Various phytoremediation techniques include phytoextraction, phytostabilization, rhizodegradation, rhizofiltration, phytodegradation, and phytovolatilization, each has unique applications, advantages, and challenges, offering a comprehensive array of strategies for remediation [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib4)].

The uptake of metals and organic compounds is a complex process influenced by the properties of the contaminants, the species of the plants, the characteristics of the soil, and the prevailing environmental conditions [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib5)]. Plants can absorb metals passively through diffusion or actively via specialized transporter proteins, and produce chelating agents to facilitate this uptake. Similarly, plants passively absorb hydrophobic organic compounds while actively transporting water-soluble ones. Once absorbed, contaminants undergo metabolism and detoxification within the plant and may be stored or translocated to different tissues [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib6)]. Investigating the mechanisms of pollutant uptake in plants can enhance remediation efforts.

Strategic plant selection involves evaluating pollutant-removal capabilities and adaptability to the local environment, thereby optimizing the success of phytoremediation. Plant species have demonstrated proficiency in this area, each utilizing distinct biochemical processes. Understanding these processes aids in selecting the most suitable species for specific remediation tasks. Different plant species exhibit varying effectiveness in remediating contaminants; for instance, _Indian mustard_ was effective for heavy metals, and _Vetiver_ grass excelled in addressing organic pollutants [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib7)].

The field of phytoremediation is evolving through innovative techniques, including genetically modified bacteria and transgenic plants, which enhance the efficiency and specificity of remediation solutions (Mello-Farias et al., 2011) A sustainable remediation assessment evaluates the long-term impacts of phytoremediation projects on environmental, economic, and social resources. Bioremediation technologies, such as microbial stimulation, genetic engineering, and the use of chelates, improve the overall efficacy of phytoremediation practices [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib8)]. Approaches such as metagenomics, proteomics, and metabolomics further enhance our understanding, opening new avenues for improving the precision and efficiency of phytoremediation [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib9)]. Nanoparticles (NPs) have emerged as potential tools in phytoremediation, serving as contaminants and as agents for remediation, which highlights their role and the challenges they present in this context. An exciting development in environmental remediation is the integration of phytoremediation with bioenergy production, utilizing plants for pollution cleanup while generating renewable energy [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib10)].

This review examines phytoremediation, its mechanisms, strategies, and recent advancements. It aims to clarify how phytoremediation functions, the various strategies to enhance its efficacy, and the challenges to its widespread implementation. Overall, this analysis provides a nuanced understanding of phytoremediation's potential for sustainable environmental management, guiding future research and practical applications of plants as eco-friendly tools for cleanup.

2. Heavy metals and uptake mechanism
------------------------------------

Heavy metals are persistent and toxic, posing risks to ecosystems and human health. Both industrial and natural activities contribute to their mobility, resulting in soil and water contamination, particularly in mining regions. Their persistence facilitates bioaccumulation and biomagnification, highlighting the need for effective remediation strategies. The solubility and bioavailability of heavy metals are influenced by their chemical forms and soil properties, which are affected by factors such as pH and microbial activity. Plants and microorganisms enhance the availability of these metals through root exudation, the secretion of metal-mobilizing compounds, and enzyme activity, thereby facilitating plant uptake.

Heavy metal accumulation in plants involves several processes, including mobilization, uptake, transport, and sequestration. Metals are absorbed at the root surface and enter cells through either passive or active pathways. Within root cells, they form complexes with chelators like organic acids and are sequestered in cell walls or vacuoles (Peuke and Rennenberg, 2005). These metals are subsequently transported to the shoots via the xylem. In leaves, they are stored in cell walls or vacuoles to prevent accumulation in the cytosol [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib11)].

Heavy metals interact with cellular biomolecules, leading to an increase in reactive oxygen species. Metal cations form complexes with phytochelatins, which sequester these metals in vacuoles, isolating toxic metals from essential cellular processes [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib11)]. Metallothioneins bind to nutrient metals and protect plants from harmful ions. Cysteine-rich peptides, such as metallothioneins and phytochelatins sequester metals by forming bonds with sulfur residues. Increasing the concentrations of these peptides can enhance metal accumulation in plants [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib12)]. Phytochelatins detoxify heavy metals and are synthesized by enzymes such as γ-glutamylcysteine synthetase (γECS) and glutamine synthetase [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib13)]. Engineered phytoremediation systems utilize bacterial genes to convert mercury into less toxic forms. Ferric Chelate Reductase (FRO2) facilitates iron uptake by reducing Fe(III) to Fe(II). Balancing the activities of metal transporters is crucial for ensuring proper metal distribution, storage, and utilization in metalloproteins and metalloenzymes [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib14)].

Heavy metal uptake and translocation involve various molecules, including metal ion transporters and complexing agents. Specialized transporters in the root cell membranes mediate the influx and efflux of metals [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib5)]. Genetic and molecular techniques have identified several genes involved in metal transport, such as heavy metal ATPases (HMAs), natural resistance-associated macrophage proteins (Nramps), the cation diffusion facilitator (CDF) family, the zinc-regulated, iron-regulated transporter-like protein (ZIP) family, and cation/proton antiporters (CPAs) [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib15)]. For example, OsZIP9 in rice enhances zinc uptake under zinc-limited conditions [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib16)]. HMA proteins and metal tolerance proteins (MTPs) play crucial roles in heavy metal transport and accumulation in plants [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib17)].

3. Organic pollutants and uptake mechanisms
-------------------------------------------

Organic pollutants present significant challenges due to their complex structures and resistance to degradation, leading to environmental persistence and health issues such as cancer, reproductive disorders, immune system alterations, hypertension, and diabetes. Hydrophobic organic compounds (HOCs) strongly bind to soil particles, which reduces their concentration in water [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib3)]. The bioavailability of HOCs is influenced by their distribution between soil and water, with soil organic matter serving as a sorbent. Microbial activity plays a crucial role in the degradation and fate of HOCs. Additionally, different plant species exhibit varying abilities to absorb and transport organic compounds, a process influenced by root exudates, mycorrhizal associations, and rhizospheric bacteria that enhance HOC degradation [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib18)].

Plant roots often serve as the primary tissue for encountering organic pollutants, making root absorption the predominant mechanism for uptake. This is followed by diffusion, which facilitates the uptake of organic pollutants and their subsequent translocation to various parts of the plant [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib19)]. The detoxification process involves several key steps, including chemical modification, functionalization, conjugation, and compartmentalization, with enzymes playing a crucial role in either completely mineralizing pollutants or partially degrading them into stable intermediates [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib20)]. The conjugation of pollutants with endogenous compounds, such as amino acids and proteins, aids in their sequestration in vacuoles and cell walls. Active transport mechanisms, including the ATP-dependent glutathione pump, facilitate the movement of these conjugates [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib21)]. This system recognizes a variety of compounds, including oxidized glutathione, glutathione conjugates of organic compounds, diverse high-molecular-weight toxic organic xenobiotics, and peptide-metal complexes such as phytochelatins. In plants, conjugation and compartmentalization are critical steps in the detoxification of xenobiotics. Once inside the cells, xenobiotic compounds are primarily metabolized by xenome enzymes, which reduce their toxicity in the form of conjugates before compartmentalization or excretion [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib12)].

4. Soil, water, and air: phytoremediation essentials
----------------------------------------------------

Phytoremediation is a sustainable approach for mitigating pollution in soil, water, and air by using plants to absorb, degrade, or transform contaminants. This method effectively addresses pollutants like heavy metals, organic compounds, and radioactive elements, providing a versatile alternative to traditional remediation techniques. Its efficiency is influenced by several factors, including plant species, soil characteristics, root exudates, the type of contaminant, and plant-microbe interactions within the rhizosphere.

It is an effective and sustainable method for cleaning up contaminated soil. This process can be enhanced through the use of soil amendments, which improve the efficiency of contaminant removal. Additionally, mycorrhizal fungi have been shown to enhance the uptake and translocation of heavy metals from the soil into plants. Certain plant species, such as _Viola baoshanensis, Sedum alfredii, Vertiveria zizanioides, Dianthus chinensis, Rumex_ K-1 (_Rumex patientia_×_R. timschmicus_), _Rumex crispus,_ and _Rumex acetosa,_ have shown notable potential for remediating heavy metals in soils [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib22)]. For example, _Chenopodium ambrosioides_ exhibited resistance to Cd with a growth tolerance index ranging from 117.64% to 194.11% for overall growth and from 188.23% to 264.70% for shoots growth [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib226)]. In contrast, _Brassica juncea_ showed a tolerance index of 87.4% for shoots and 89.6% for roots in contaminated soils [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib23)]. Hernández et al. [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib24)] compared the phytoextraction capacities of _Lactuca sativa_ and _Lolium perenne_ for removing Ni, Fe, and Co from soils contaminated by the galvanoplastic industry. They found that _Lactuca sativa_ accumulated more Ni and Co, while _Lolium perenne_ was more effective at accumulating Fe. The study suggested selecting plant species based on the specific metal targeted for optimal remediation, noting that soil pH influences metal concentrations and plant survival. Pandey et al. [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib25)] observed a positive correlation between metal accumulation in _Typha latifolia_ and soil metal concentrations, indicating that higher concentrations in the soil and longer exposure durations resulted in increased accumulation of Zn, Mn, Cu, Pb, Cd, Cr, and Ni in the plant. Liu et al. [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib26)] identified plant species such as Purple Coneflower, Aster Callistephus, Fawn, Fire Phoenix, and Alfalfa as effective for remediating petroleum-contaminated soils.

A study by Ning et al. [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib27)] found that using a compounded activation agent derived from fruit residue enhanced the phytoextraction of Pb by _Sedum alfredii_, increasing Pb uptake in the plant's tissues and demonstrating a promising method for improving phytoremediation efficiency in Pb-contaminated soils. Plant-growth-promoting microbes with ACC (1-aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylate) deaminase enhanced germination and growth, while arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi (AMF) improved nutrient uptake [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib28)]. Dhaliwal et al. [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib29)] noted that adding a chelating agent to the soil improved the phytoextraction of Cd uptake by plants, resulting in a 15% increase in metal uptake as ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) levels in the soil rose from 1 to 2 mg/kg. Shinta et al. [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib30)] concluded that citric acid was more effective than EDTA in improving soil conditions for phytoremediation by lowering the soil's pH and promoting a beneficial microbial community, which enhanced plant root growth and facilitated the uptake and accumulation of contaminants.

Water pollution is a pressing global issue that can be sustainably addressed through phytoremediation, which involves treating contaminated water bodies, wetlands, and industrial effluents. Aquatic plants such as water hyacinth [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib31)], duckweed [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib32)], and water lettuce [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib33)] have demonstrated the ability to accumulate heavy metals, degrade organic pollutants, and remove excess nutrients, thereby contributing to water purification. Zhu et al. [[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib34)] emphasized that excessive nutrients, such as nitrogen and phosphorus in water bodies lead to eutrophication, causing algal blooms and oxygen depletion. They reported the effectiveness of aquatic plants like water hyacinth and duckweed in reducing nutrient levels through uptake and assimilation, which helps to mitigate the adverse effects on water quality in affected aquatic environments. According to Qin et al. [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib33)], water hyacinth effectively mitigated carbon, water lettuce successfully reduced nitrogen, and duckweed efficiently removed phosphorus from contaminated ponds.

Ansari et al. [[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib35)] discovered that floating aquatic phytoremediators, such as Azolla, Eichhornia, Lemna, Spirodela, Wolfia, and Wolffiella significantly removed water pollutants through the bioaccumulation of heavy metals and organic contaminants in their tissues. Uysal and Taner [[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib36)] observed that metal accumulation in plants increased with initial metal concentrations ranging from 0 to 50 mg/L, but decreased when concentrations rose from 50 to 100 mg/L. This decline led to plant wilting due to metal toxicity, which may have been exacerbated by the transpiration of metal ions from the roots to the surrounding solution, and negatively impacted plant survival. Studies by Jang et al. [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib37)] and Wang et al. [[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib38)] demonstrated that wetland plants, such as _Phragmites australis_ and _Typha latifolia_ marginally removed nanoparticles like Ag, Cu, and Zn from wastewater by accumulating them in their tissues, highlighting their potential for phytoremediation in treating wastewater contaminated with metallic nanoparticles. Conversely, Xu et al. [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib39)] showed that duckweed effectively removed PAHs from water bodies, with the plant's associated microorganisms degrading PAHs in contaminated water and reducing their concentrations. _Vetiveria zizanioide_ s, a perennial bunchgrass, exhibited a 93% removal rate of the textile dye Remazol Red from wastewater, while a plant consortium with _Ipomoea aquatica_ further enhanced textile dye removal by increasing dye-degrading enzymes in its root and shoot tissues [[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib40)].

Air quality significantly impacts human health and ecosystem well-being, and plants can purify air by absorbing and metabolizing airborne pollutants. Schaub et al. [[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405844024147147#bib41)] investigated the use of trees for the phytoremediation of air pollutants, examining the

Phytoremediation: Mechanisms, plant selection and enhancement by natural and synthetic agents
=============================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Arjun Kafle a, Anil Timilsina b, Asmita Gautam c, Kaushik Adhikari d, Anukul Bhattarai e, Niroj Aryal f

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •[Phytoremediation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/phytoremediation "Learn more about Phytoremediation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is an economical and sustainable technology for environmental cleanup. 
*   •Plant species known to utilize different phyto-mechanisms during phytoremediation of diverse pollutants has been presented. 
*   •Different aids to overcome the limitations of phytoremediation are described. 
*   •Strengths and limitations inherent with the phytoremediation technique is supported by recent research. 

Abstract
--------

With current intensive agriculture practices and industrialization, pollution of natural resources like land and water with heavy metals, organic pollutants, radionuclides, pesticides, and fertilizers has become a major concern. Phytoremediation is a cost-effective and environmentally friendly technique that utilizes plants to immobilize, uptake, reduce toxicity, stabilize, or degrade the compounds that are released into the environment from different sources. Studies have shown that heavy metals, organic contaminants, radionuclides, antibiotics, and pesticides can be remediated using plants. Though phytoremediation has been practiced since decades, it is still an emerging technology. This review article summarizes existing information and synthesizes the recent findings on plant species suitable for use in phytoremediation through utilizing different mechanisms, aids that can enhance the efficiency of phytoremediation processes, and strengths and limitations that comes with the application of this technique. Diverse plants remediate different pollutants at different rates through one or multiple mechanisms. The limitations of phytoremediation can be overcome by using several aids including natural and chemical amendments, genetic engineering and natural microbial stimulation. Given the low-cost of phytoremediation compared to conventional technology and sustainability associated with plants and use of renewable energy, phytoremediation can be a reliable solution for a sustainable and economical remediation of soil and water from the organic and inorganic pollutants.

1. Introduction
---------------

Rapid rise in global population accompanied by ensuing demands for food, water, energy, timber, and other ecosystem services has led to severe stresses on the [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/natural-resources "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the environment ([Kamran et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0141); [Saleem et al., 2020c](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0235); [Zaheer et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0298)). [Anthropogenic activities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/anthropogenic-activity "Learn more about Anthropogenic activities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") including the agricultural intensification practices ([Mózner et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0192)), speedy urbanization, and rapid industrialization ([Cherniwchan,2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0049); [Wu et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0289)) are causing serious environmental pollution by [heavy metals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/heavy-metal "Learn more about heavy metals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HMs) and metalloids ([Anyakora et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0015); [He et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0118); [Su,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0261)), radionuclides ([Hu et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0125); [Prakash et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0215)), organic compounds ([Afzal et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0003)), agrochemicals ([Malik et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0177); [Sunitha et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0265)), and oil spills ([Ron & Rosenberg,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0228)). Mining activities, disposal of effluents from industries and [residential areas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/residential-area "Learn more about residential areas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), heavy use of fertilizers and pesticides, and irrigation of contaminated water have led to contamination of soils ([Awad et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0020); [Tang et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0267); [Teng et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0270)). Mining leads to disturbance of many soil properties, including pH, electrical conductivity, and cation exchange capacity ([Saleem et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0233)). Consequently, HMs such as Copper (Cu), Lead (Pb), Zinc (Zn), Nickel (Ni), Cadmium (Cd), Chromium (Cr), Iron (Fe), Manganese (Mn) and organic pollutants such as hydrocarbons, aromatic compounds, phenols, organo-chlorinated compounds, pesticides have been found at elevated concentrations in the soil and sediments ([Gutiérrez-Ginés et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0111); [Liu et al., 2017b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0169); [Song et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0254)).

HMs accumulation in soil affects [soil microbial population](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-microbial-populations "Learn more about soil microbial population from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), processes and their activity ([Huang et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0126); [Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)). The effects of HMs on microbial population changes and enzymatic activities are dependent upon the type of HMs and their chemical affinity ([Karaca et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0142)). For examples, cadmium (Cd) affects [ureases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/urease "Learn more about ureases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), proteases and basic phosphatases ([Lorenz et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0170)); lead (Pb) affects [catalases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/catalase "Learn more about catalases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), ureases, [invertase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/invertase "Learn more about invertase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [acid phosphatase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/acid-phosphatase "Learn more about acid phosphatase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Belyaeva et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0032)); Arsenic (As) affects [sulfatase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/sulfatase "Learn more about sulfatase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and phosphatase ([Speir et al., 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0258)); and copper (Cu) restricts b-glucosidase ([Geiger et al., 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0097)). High chromium (VI) concentration in soil exhibits distinct toxicity characteristics causing negative effects in soil microbial cell metabolism ([Huang et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0126)).

The [ingestion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/ingestion "Learn more about ingestion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of food crops contaminated with HMs can lead to [bioaccumulation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bioaccumulation "Learn more about bioaccumulation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of HMs and subsequent biomagnification along the higher [trophic levels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/trophic-level "Learn more about trophic levels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the food chain can threaten both animal and human health ([Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)). HMs accumulation in human body can cause serious complications in human health such as Itai-itai disease due to chronic Cd exposure ([Pan et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0205)); kidney dysfunction and anemia due to Zn ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)); [mucosa](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/mucous-membrane "Learn more about mucosa from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") corrosion, hepatic system failure and [central nervous system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/central-nervous-system "Learn more about central nervous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") damage due to Cu ([Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)); [skin irritation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/skin-irritation "Learn more about skin irritation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [nervous system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nervous-system "Learn more about nervous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") complications due to Ni ([Argun et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0016)); cardiovascular problems and damage to nervous system due to Pb ([Kazemipour et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0144); [Singh & Kalamdhad,2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0249)); abortion, nervous breakdown and skin rashes due to Hg ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)); cancer due to Cr ([Zohdi et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0304)); and carcinogenic and [immune system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/immune-system "Learn more about immune system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") dysfunction due to As ([Duruibe et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0072)).

Among different organic contaminants, chlorinated solvents, petroleum hydrocarbons explosives ([Alkorta & Garbisu,2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0011)), polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), pyrene, and trinitrotoluene (TNT) ([Chekol & Vough,2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0045)) are major soil contaminants. Pesticides that often contaminate soils include [atrazine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/atrazine "Learn more about atrazine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [dieldrin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/dieldrin "Learn more about dieldrin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [hexachlorobenzene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/hexachlorobenzene "Learn more about hexachlorobenzene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), dichlorodiphenyltrichlorethane (DDT), and 1,2,4- trichlorobenzene ([Chiou et al., 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0050)). These pollutants seriously affect the [soil biota](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-biota "Learn more about soil biota from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), mineral nutrients availability, and soil productivity because of their persistence in soil for long period of time and toxicity.

Like soils, [water resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/water-resources "Learn more about water resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have also been contaminated by different pollutants. Urbanization and industrialization activities release toxic heavy metals through the sewage, runoff and effluents contributing to largest share of factors for water contamination ([Masindi & Muedi,2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0182); [Parveen et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0206); [Saleem et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0233)). Over 80% of world's wastewater reach to environment without treatment causing nearly 58% of health issues like diarrhea, which is a major cause for early child dealth ([Connor et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0054)). Among metals, Cu, Ni, Pb, Cd, Cr, U, and Zn frequently contaminate water resources ([Prasad & Freitas,2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0216)). The HMs degrade the quality of water and can lead to water-borne health hazards through heavy metal accumulation in the food chain ([Wongsasuluk et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0287)). Apart from HMs, organic pollutants contaminate water resources either by erosion, seepage, sludge and effluents from municipal waste and industries, and runoff from agricultural practices ([Diez,2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0064)) and mining ([Tanhan et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0268)). These contaminants can accumulate over time and negatively affect the aquatic as well as [terrestrial ecosystems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/terrestrial-ecosystem "Learn more about terrestrial ecosystems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Remediation of the soil and water pollutants for sustaining ecosystem processes and functions is one of the major challenges our society [faces](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/face "Learn more about faces from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") today. Many physical, chemical, and biological approaches have been employed for remediating the environmental pollution, however, their applications are limited due to the cost and labor requirements, safety hazards, and risks to ecosystems ([Ali et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0010)). An alternative technique, [phytoremediation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/phytoremediation "Learn more about phytoremediation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), can be an effective technique and is gaining popularity, acceptance, and implementation.

Phytoremediation technique utilizes plants to remediate the contaminated media including soil and water. Phytoremediation is an economically and environmentally favorable technique as it utilizes [green plants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/viridiplantae "Learn more about green plants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to contain, sequester or detoxify contaminants from contaminated soil and water ([Ashraf et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0019)). Phytoremediation utilizes many mechanisms including degradation (rhizo-degradation, phytodegradation), accumulation (phytoextraction, rhizofiltration), dissipation (phytovolatilization), and immobilization (hydraulic control and phytostabilization) to degrade, remove, or immobilize the pollutants ([Pivetz,2001b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0214)). Depending upon the contaminants, plants utilize one or more of these mechanisms to reduce their concentrations from soil and water. For examples, plants uptake and accumulate the HMs in their tissues ([Mahar et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0176)) and degrade the organic pollutants ([Saleem et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0234)) reducing their toxicity from soil and water resources.

Different plant uses different methods or their combinations to remediate soil and water depending upon the contaminant types, forms, and media. Contaminated ground water can be remediated through phytodegradation, phytovolatilization, rhizo-filtration, rhizo-degradation and phytodegradation. Surface and wastewater contaminations can be treated by [rhizofiltration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/rhizofiltration "Learn more about rhizofiltration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), phytodegradation or rhizodegradation. Soil, sediments or sludges contaminations are remediated through phytoextraction, phytodegradations, phytostabilization, rhizodegradation or phytovolatilization.

Ideal plant species for phytoremediation purpose should be hardy in nature, high biomass-producing, tolerant to toxic effects of metals and contaminants, easy to cultivate, have high absorption capacity, and be non-attractive for herbivory ([Adesodun et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0002); [Sakakibara et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0231); [Shabani & Sayadi,2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0243)). In many cases, plant species cannot perform well and need aids to enhance phytoremediation. Such aids include soil amendments like biochar ([Paz-Ferreiro et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0207)), Ethylene Diamine Tetraacetic Acid (EDTA) ([Shahid et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0244)), endophytic bacteria ([Afzal et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0003)), [Arbuscular mycorrhiza](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/arbuscular-mycorrhiza "Learn more about Arbuscular mycorrhiza from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Gaur & Adholeya,2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0095)) or even [transgenic plants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/transgenic-plant "Learn more about transgenic plants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Abhilash et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0001); [Kawahigashi,2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0143)). They promote the phytoremediation process either by reducing the toxicity of pollutants or increasing the availability of pollutants or promoting the growth of plants.

Previous articles have provided information regarding techniques ([Jadia & Fulekar,2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0132); [Sharma & Pathak,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0245); [Willey,2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0285)), plants used ([Banuelos et al., 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0027); [Cheng,2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0047); [Pilon-Smits & Pilon,2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0211)), factors affecting the processes ([Antoniadis et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0014); [Sheoran et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0246)), aids or stimulators ([Coninx et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0053); [Hovsepyan & Greipsson,2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0123); [Ullah et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0273)) and are either specific to soil ([Belouchrani et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0031); [Romeh,2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0227); [Waigi et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0278)) or water resources ([Nazir et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0197); [Rezania et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0226); [Ugya & Imam,2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666765722000394#bib0272)). enriched_points.expanded_content: Research focuses on refining phytoremediation methods, enhancing efficiency through agronomic practices and hormone optimization for better uptake of contaminants, as seen in recent studies.
Investigating diverse plant species may yield novel candidates for phytoremediation, improving pollutant uptake and resistance, thus expanding the range of effective remediation options.

Cross-disciplinary partnerships with tech sectors can integrate advanced tools, like genetically modified organisms and nanoparticles, enhancing phytoremediation effectiveness and monitoring capabilities.
Expanding phytoremediation applications across varied environments—including urban, agricultural, and industrial areas—can address diverse pollutant types and improve ecosystem restoration efforts.
